metadata.description: RelBench rel-stack dataset for JRN estimation experiments

examples.input: {"Id": 9, "PostId": 6, "UserId": 957, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "UserDisplayName": null, "Text": "http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chebychev%27s_inequality  This applies regardless of the distribution.", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T20:49:38.013000"}

{"Id": 25, "PostId": 9, "UserId": 3314, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "UserDisplayName": null, "Text": "If a known event is going to change the distribution it is essential to recalibrate.  In a mass spectrometer the device becomes more accurate as it adjusts itself to the compound being analyzed.  This is a well known method to increase accuracy while supporting a large variety of inputs.", "CreationDate": "2009-04-12T08:13:05.517000"}

{"Id": 8, "PostId": 6, "UserId": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "UserDisplayName": "David Thornley", "Text": "It's not at all hard to prove that there has to be at least one data point within one standard deviation (inclusive) of the mean.  Any outlier big enough to pull the mean way out is going to enlarge the standard deviation a lot.", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T17:47:21.783000"}

{"Id": 21, "PostId": 9, "UserId": 3314, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "UserDisplayName": null, "Text": "The system has to know that it is being calibrated and bootstrapping itself.  Typical techniques are to seed the first values with the initial value plus or minus ten percent.  When the second value arrives the seeded values are the first and second values interleaved.", "CreationDate": "2009-04-12T07:53:44.953000"}

{"Id": 0, "PostId": 1, "UserId": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "UserDisplayName": "Jason Cohen", "Text": "Using a rule like \"biggest 10%\" doesn't make sense. What if there are no outliers? The 10% rule would eliminate some data anyway. Unacceptable.", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:45:19.280000"}

{"Id": 8, "PostId": 6, "UserId": null, "PostHistoryTypeId": 2, "UserDisplayName": "Jason Cohen", "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "RevisionGUID": "0418121d-2c22-4e4d-9d2b-0373370a9fe3", "Text": "A statistically sensible approach is to use a [standard deviation][1] cut-off.

For example, remove any results +/-3 standard deviations.

Using a rule like "biggest 10%" doesn't make sense.  What if there are no outliers?  The 10% rule would eliminate some data anyway.  Unacceptable.


  [1]: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation", "Comment": null, "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:44:30.840000"}

{"Id": 16, "PostId": 12, "UserId": null, "PostHistoryTypeId": 2, "UserDisplayName": "ozan", "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "RevisionGUID": "2034d882-59f1-4d6e-b469-d71a9633dd30", "Text": "You need to have some idea of expected variation or distribution, if you want to be able to exclude certain (higher) instances of variation as erroneous. For instance, if you can approximate the distribution of the \"average times\" result to a normal (Gaussian) distribution, then you can do what ojblass suggested and exclude those results that exhibit a variation that is greater than some multiple of the standard deviation (which can be calculated on the fly alongside your running average). If yo...", "Comment": null, "CreationDate": "2009-04-12T07:53:27.777000"}

{"Id": 0, "PostId": 0, "UserId": 76449, "PostHistoryTypeId": 1, "UserDisplayName": "Tawani", "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "RevisionGUID": "f2d5ed7a-52d0-4831-8056-d337bd96ac72", "Text": "What is an Average that does not include outliers?", "Comment": null, "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:21:12.103000"}

{"Id": 11, "PostId": 9, "UserId": null, "PostHistoryTypeId": 2, "UserDisplayName": "Edward Z. Yang", "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "RevisionGUID": "2d7b38bf-bc28-44eb-a6fc-794d3ab306f1", "Text": "We have a daemon that reads in data from some sensors, and among the things it calculates (besides simply just reporting the state) is the average time it takes for the sensors to change from one value to another. It keeps a running average of 64 datapoints, and assumes that runtime is fairly constant. Unfortunately, as demonstrated by the below graph, the input data isn't the most pristine: (Each line...", "Comment": null, "CreationDate": "2009-04-12T07:24:17.007000"}

{"Id": 0, "OwnerUserId": 76449, "PostTypeId": 1, "ParentId": null, "OwnerDisplayName": "Tawani", "Title": "What do you call an average that does not include outliers?", "Tags": "", "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 3.0", "Body": "

What do you call an average that does not include outliers?

For example if you have a set:

{90,89,92,91,5} avg = 73.4

but excluding the outlier (5) we have

{90,89,92,91(,5)} avg = 90.5

How do you describe this average in statistics?

", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:21:12.103000"}

{"Id": 1, "OwnerUserId": 957, "PostTypeId": 2, "ParentId": 0, "OwnerDisplayName": "dsimcha", "Title": null, "Tags": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 4.0", "Body": "

It's called the trimmed mean. Basically what you do is compute the mean of the middle 80% of your data, ignoring the top and bottom 10%. Of course, these numbers can vary, but that's the general idea.

\n", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:24:31.740000"}

{"Id": 8, "OwnerUserId": null, "PostTypeId": 2, "ParentId": 0, "OwnerDisplayName": "Nick Fortescue", "Title": null, "Tags": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "Body": "

The most common way of having a Robust (the usual word meaning resistant to bad data) average is to use the median. This is just the middle value in the sorted list (of half way between the middle two values), so for your example it would be 90.5 = half way between 90 and 91.

\n\n

If you want to get really into robust statistics (such as robust estimates of standard deviation etc) I would recomme...", "CreationDate": "2009-02-13T09:22:19.977000"}

{"Id": 5, "OwnerUserId": null, "PostTypeId": 2, "ParentId": 0, "OwnerDisplayName": "mvrak", "Title": null, "Tags": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 2.5", "Body": "

There is no official name because of the various mechanisms, such as Q test, used to get rid of outliers.

Removing outliers is called trimming.

No program I have ever used has average() with an integrated trim()

", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:31:27.840000"}

{"Id": 3, "OwnerUserId": 884, "PostTypeId": 2, "ParentId": 0, "OwnerDisplayName": "Jason S", "Title": null, "Tags": null, "ContentLicense": "CC BY-SA 3.0", "Body": "

I don't know if it has a name, but you could easily come up with a number of algorithms to reject outliers:

  1. Find all numbers between the 10th and 90th percentiles (do this by sorting then rejecting the first $N/10$ and last $N/10$ numbers) and take the mean value of the remaining values.

  2. Sort values, reject high and low values as long as by doing so, the mean/standard deviation change more than $X\%$.

  3. Sort values, reject high and low values as ...", "CreationDate": "2009-02-02T14:25:44.200000"}
